```
findings — Sisal's fifth dialect and first new adapter since libSQL. The
`"mysql"` dialect is no longer merely latent: after v0.6 it **renders correct
SQL or throws a typed guard for every construct** (quoting, placeholders,
`ilike`→`LIKE`, locking, the dialect-mapped upsert + `excluded()`; guards on
`RETURNING`, `UPDATE … FROM`, `DELETE … USING`, data-modifying CTEs,
`distinctOn`, array operators, `FULL JOIN`, `filter()`, the date helpers). v0.7
turns that correct dialect into a real, tested adapter — and lifts the guards
with real mysql renderings (`filter()`/date helpers in B2, multi-table mutation
joins in B10).

## Goal

Ship `@sisal/mysql` at OLTP feature-parity with the other adapters **wherever
MySQL supports it**, with the divergences (upsert, `RETURNING`, types) handled
explicitly — not silently mis-rendered.

## Why this is low-friction architecturally

Sisal's adapter conventions make a new engine an additive change: **every
adapter has the same internal shape** (`<adapter>/orm/` = dialect · executor ·
driver · pool · errors; `<adapter>/migrate/` = driver · history · migrator ·
ddl), the real driver is imported **lazily** with an **injectable executor** (so
unit tests stay network-free), and the CLI's `sisal init` targets are a single
`INIT_TARGETS` registry — **adding a database target is one registry entry**. So
`@sisal/mysql` slots into existing seams rather than reworking core.

## Scope — build

- **`@sisal/mysql` package** mirroring `@sisal/pg`/`@sisal/sqlite`:
  `MYSQL_DIALECT = "mysql"`, executor, lazy driver + pool, errors; and
  `migrate/` (driver, history, migrator, `ddl`).
- **Driver integration (B4)** — wire the drivers **chosen and benchmarked in
  v0.6 C6**: `npm:mysql2` as the lazily-imported default with
  `supportBigNumbers` + `bigNumberStrings` **mandatory** (its default `BIGINT`
  decode is silently lossy past 2⁵³), `npm:mariadb` as the lazy opt-in (the
  postgres.js pattern), behind the injectable-executor seam. Executor value
  normalization per C4: JSON parse-on-read (MariaDB returns text — its `JSON` is
  a `LONGTEXT` alias), the `TINYINT(1)` → boolean decision, `Buffer` →
  `Uint8Array`.
- ~~**Correct/extend the latent `"mysql"` render path**~~ — **done in v0.6**: C1
  pinned quoting/placeholders/`ilike`/locking, C2 mapped the upsert, and the
  C3/C5 guard sweep corrected every `dialectGuard` set — **no construct renders
  wrong SQL under `mysql`**. ~~What remains is **lifting** two typed throws with
  real renderings (B2).~~ **Done — B2 (2026-07-01)**: `filter()` renders the
  `CASE WHEN` fallback and the date helpers render their mysql variants; see the
  task detail.
- ~~**Upsert**~~ — **decided and implemented in v0.6 C2**: `onConflict*`
  dialect-maps to `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` in core, with `excluded()` →
  `values(col)` (target validated-not-rendered; conflict `where` throws; DO
  NOTHING → no-op self-assignment). The adapter **consumes** this; the 8.0.19+
  `AS new` row alias is variant/version work on the B1 axis.
- **`RETURNING` (B7)** — the render path already throws a typed guard (v0.6 C3;
  MySQL 8/9 has **no** `RETURNING`, and MariaDB's is per-statement and
  per-version). The adapter work is the execution strategy: a fetch-by-key
  fallback in the executor, and/or lighting MariaDB
  `INSERT`/`DELETE …
  RETURNING` per-statement through the B1 capability
  descriptor.
- **`generateMysqlUpStatements` (B5)** — additive-only DDL implemented **to the
  probe-verified C4 spec** ([mysql-ddl-mapping.md](mysql-ddl-mapping.md)):
  table-level FKs only (inline `REFERENCES` is silently ignored by MySQL),
  paren-wrapped expression defaults, **no** `ENGINE`/`CHARSET` clause, no
  `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS`, and the three generation-time validations
  (`AUTO_INCREMENT` placement, no bare `TEXT` keys, partial/functional-index
  rejection); destructive diffs withheld like the other generators. Version
  floor: MySQL ≥ 8.0.16, MariaDB ≥ 10.10.
- ~~**Type mapping & round-trips**~~ — **designed and probe-verified in v0.6
  C4** (the full kind→type table incl. `BOOLEAN`=`TINYINT(1)`, `timestamp` →
  `DATETIME(6)`, `timestamptz` → `TIMESTAMP(6)` with the 2038 caveat, `.array()`
  → `JSON`, `bytea` → `LONGBLOB`); v0.7 implements it in B4/B5.
- **Advisory-lock equivalent (B6)** — `GET_LOCK`/`RELEASE_LOCK` (named locks)
  for the migration history store (parity with pg's `pg_advisory_lock`), as
  recorded in C4 — also the lock the v0.6 A2 ETL contract expects.
- **CLI (B9)** — one `INIT_TARGETS` entry (id/aliases/dialect/connection hints).
- **Tests, docs, matrix (B8)** — fill the reserved `"mysql"` slot in the
  consolidated integration structure: an `IntegrationTarget` descriptor
  (capabilities/value shapes fall straight out of the C4/C5 probes) + a
  `mysql_family_scenarios.ts` list, gated `SISAL_MYSQL_IT=1` (Docker
  `mysql:8.4` + `mariadb:11`, the C-series probe servers),
  `docs/mysql-compatibility.md`, and a **fifth column in the feature matrix**
  (test-backed, per the existing guard).

## Task detail (Workstream B)

- **B1 — the `(engine, variant, version)` dialect axis. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).**
  The C5 dialect-identity decision is now core code, landed **before the v0.8 IR
  freeze** as required, pinned by `packages/orm/dialect_identity_test.ts`:
  - **`DialectIdentity`** (`{ dialect, variant?, version? }`) — `SqlDialect`
    stays the render key; a bare dialect means "base engine, version unknown".
    Accepted everywhere a dialect was
    (`renderSql`/`renderToPlan`/`normalizeSqlInput`, non-breaking), carried by
    the `Database` facade as `dialectIdentity`
    (`createDatabase({ dialect, variant, version })`, inherited by transaction
    facades) — the adapter (B4) fills it from the server handshake.
  - **`dialectGuard` generalized, declaratively** — guard chunks stay
    serializable data (the IR-freeze constraint): targets may narrow to one
    `variant`, and `unless` refinements (`{ variant?, minVersion? }`) lift a
    guard per identity. **Fail closed:** an unknown version never satisfies
    `minVersion` — capabilities light up only when the adapter has identified
    the server. `compareServerVersions` (dotted-numeric prefix, suffix-blind:
    `"11.8.8-MariaDB-ubu2404"`) is exported for adapters.
  - **First real consumer:** the `RETURNING` guard split per statement kind with
    the probe-verified MariaDB floors — `INSERT` 10.5+, `DELETE` 10.0.5+,
    `UPDATE` 13.0+ — so a `mariadb` identity renders
    `INSERT`/`DELETE …
    RETURNING` while `UPDATE … RETURNING` stays guarded
    until 13.0, and plain `mysql` keeps the C3 behavior unchanged (dormant until
    B4 wires the identity).
  - **Snapshot half:** `SisalSchemaSnapshot` gains optional
    `dialectVariant`/`dialectVersion` (additive — older snapshots unchanged,
    `SCHEMA_SNAPSHOT_VERSION` stays 2), accepted by `createSchemaSnapshot` for
    B5's variant-aware DDL decisions.
  - Open for B4: the identity is static at facade construction, but adapters
    connect lazily — the connect path needs a resolve-then-build step (or a
    re-identify hook) to fill variant/version from `select version()`.
- **B2 — mysql renderings for `filter()` + the date helpers. ✅ Done
  (2026-07-01).** The last two typed throws are lifted with real renderings,
  **executed live on MySQL 8.4.10 and MariaDB 11.8.8 with semantic checks**
  (identical values to the pg/sqlite behavior) before pinning in
  `packages/orm/mysql_dialect_test.ts`:
  - **`filter()` → `agg(CASE WHEN cond THEN operand END)`.** The compose-only
    fragment IR cannot introspect an opaque `sum("col")`, so the exported
    aggregates (`count`/`countDistinct`/`sum`/`avg`/`min`/`max`) now stamp
    rebuild metadata into a WeakMap (the `RESULT_METADATA` precedent — frozen
    fragments and the public surface untouched); `filter()` rebuilds the mysql
    variant from it (`count(*)` counts a literal `1`; `countDistinct` keeps
    `DISTINCT`; NULLs from non-matching rows are skipped by every aggregate,
    matching `FILTER` semantics — verified with live sums/counts/ averages). A
    hand-written `` sql`…` `` aggregate has no metadata and keeps the typed
    throw — an honest bound, and a data point for the v0.8 introspectable-IR
    question.
  - **Date helpers**: `dateTrunc` → `DATE_FORMAT` (string result like the SQLite
    family; MySQL minutes are `%i`), `now()` → `NOW(6)` (microseconds, matching
    pg), `dateAdd`/`dateSub` → nested `DATE_ADD(…, INTERVAL ? unit)` with bound
    quantities, `dateBin` → `FROM_UNIXTIME(FLOOR(UNIX_TIMESTAMP(src)/N)*N)`.
    Live checks covered hour-bucket grouping, 30-minute binning, ±shifts, and
    the moving-window predicate (`gte(col, dateSub(now(), {...}))`) on both
    engines.
  - **B4 finding (recorded):** mysql2's binary protocol rejects a bound
    `LIMIT ?` on MySQL 8.4 (`Incorrect arguments to mysqld_stmt_execute`) but
    accepts it on MariaDB 11.8; identical SQL passes via the text protocol on
    both. The B4 executor must handle LIMIT/OFFSET binding (coerce, inline
    validated integers, or route through `query()`).
- **B3 — scaffold `@sisal/mysql`. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** `packages/mysql/`
  exists with the shared adapter shape (`orm/` = dialect · errors · pool ·
  executor · driver · mod), wired into the workspace, `check`, `test`, and docs
  gates, and **smoke-tested against live MySQL 8.4.10 and MariaDB 11.8.8**
  (builder round trip through the real lazy `mysql2` pool, the C2 ODKU upsert,
  transaction rollback, and MariaDB `INSERT … RETURNING` returning real rows
  through the B1 identity):
  - **Injectable executor seam**
    (`connect({ executor | pool | client |
    url })`) — the 8 unit tests run
    network-free against a recording fake, covering result mapping (row arrays
    vs mysql2's affected-rows header), transaction isolation/rollback, Temporal
    param normalization, batch atomicity, pool ownership on close, and
    end-to-end facade rendering.
  - **`mysql2/promise` imported lazily** on first connection (the
    `@sisal/libsql` discipline); the pool always sets the C6-mandated
    `supportBigNumbers` + `bigNumberStrings`.
  - **Text protocol (`query()`) is the execution path**, sidestepping the B2
    `LIMIT ?` binary-protocol finding by construction; a prepared-statement mode
    can arrive as a B4 opt-in.
  - **Identity plumbing:** `connect({ variant, version })` fills the B1
    `dialectIdentity` (exported `MARIADB_VARIANT`); adapter-side auto-detection
    from the handshake is B4/B9 work.
  - Exports are `.` and `./orm` only; `./migrate` and `./ddl` land with B5/B6.
    JSR publish dry-run passes for `@sisal/mysql@0.7.0`.
- **B4 — drivers + executor hardening. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** Verified live on
  both engines **× both drivers** (four combinations); pinned by 6 new
  network-free unit tests (14 total in the package):
  - **Identity auto-detection resolves B1's lazy-connect question:** `connect()`
    runs `select version()` once (through the executor, so it works for
    `url`/`pool`/`client` sources alike), parses it with the new
    `parseMysqlServerVersion` (MariaDB self-identifies in the string), and
    builds the facade with the filled identity. Defaults: **on** for real
    sources without an explicit `variant`/`version`, **off** for injected
    executors (the test seam); `detectVersion: false` restores a fully lazy
    connect (identity stays base — fail closed, everything version-gated stays
    guarded). Live proof: against the MariaDB container, `INSERT … RETURNING`
    returns rows **with zero configuration**; against MySQL 8.4 it stays a typed
    guard.
  - **`connect({ driver: "mariadb" })` opt-in** — the C6 performance candidate,
    adapted to the shared `MysqlPool` contract (`adaptMariadbPool`) and resolved
    through a **runtime-computed specifier**, so the LGPL connector stays a
    soft, run-time-only dependency (the postgres.js pattern; the publish
    dry-run's `unanalyzable-dynamic-import` warning is that pattern's
    signature). The mysql2-compatible `supportBigNumbers`+`bigNumberStrings`
    options hold on it too (live: bigint → `"9007199254740993"` on both
    drivers).
  - **Live-probe catch:** the MariaDB connector **JSON-serializes a plain
    `Uint8Array` parameter** (`{"0":7,…}` — silent BLOB corruption); the adapter
    now re-views binary params as Node `Buffer`s (no copy) before handing them
    over. Pinned in the unit tests; both drivers now round-trip `[7,8,9]`
    byte-identically.
  - **Value normalization:** BLOB reads are re-viewed as plain `Uint8Array`s
    (matching `columns.bytea()`'s inferred type across adapters). **`TINYINT(1)`
    stays `0`/`1`** — decision recorded below. JSON on MariaDB stays text (its
    `JSON` is a `LONGTEXT` alias and the wire protocol cannot distinguish it
    from `TEXT`, so a mechanical executor-level parse is impossible without
    guessing) — a documented variant value-shape for the B8 compat doc, exactly
    like the SQLite family's json-as-text row.
- **B5 — `generateMysqlUpStatements`. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).**
  `packages/mysql/migrate/ddl.ts` (the new `@sisal/mysql/ddl` export),
  implemented to the probe-verified C4 spec and pinned by 12 network-free unit
  tests; the **generated** output was applied live to MySQL 8.4.10 and MariaDB
  11.8.8 (kitchen-sink table + FK pair + incremental `ADD COLUMN`, with firing
  defaults and enforced FK/CHECK) — closing the loop the C4 probe opened with
  hand-written DDL:
  - **The full C4 type table** (`serial`/`bigserial` → `AUTO_INCREMENT`, `real`
    → `FLOAT`, `timestamp` → `DATETIME(6)`, `timestamptz` → `TIMESTAMP(6)` with
    explicit `NULL` when nullable, `uuid` → `CHAR(36)`, `bytea` → `LONGBLOB`,
    `json`/`jsonb`/`.array()` → `JSON`, length-less `varchar` → `VARCHAR(255)`,
    `dialectType` verbatim); unknown kinds map to `TEXT`, where the TEXT rules
    below make them fail closed.
  - **The portable-defaults rule live-verified:** expression defaults always
    paren-wrap (`DEFAULT (uuid())`, `DEFAULT (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(6))` — both
    fired on both engines), literals paren-wrap only on TEXT/BLOB/JSON-mapped
    columns (which MySQL otherwise rejects), booleans render `1`/`0`.
  - **Foreign keys table-level only, after every `CREATE TABLE`** (inline
    `REFERENCES` is silently ignored by MySQL) — the probe's child table
    deliberately sorts before its parent, proving the ordering. No
    `ENGINE`/`CHARSET` clause; `CHECK` emitted as-is (enforced live on both).
  - **Three fail-closed generation-time validations** (typed
    `ORM_DIALECT_UNSUPPORTED`): an `AUTO_INCREMENT` column must **lead** a key
    (the InnoDB rule — first column of a PK/unique/index, not mere membership),
    at most one per table, and never via `ADD COLUMN` (the additive generator
    emits no key changes); no `TEXT`/`BLOB`/`JSON`-mapped key columns (MySQL
    needs a prefix length the snapshot cannot express — the error points at
    `varchar(n)`); no partial (`WHERE`, rejected by both engines) or functional
    (MySQL-only, MariaDB rejects) indexes.
  - Same module shape as pg's generator: additive/destructive split via
    `planSchemaChangesFromDiff`, `mysql`-dialect schema objects after tables,
    schema qualifiers emitted as database qualifiers (backticked).
- **B6 — migrate wiring. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** `packages/mysql/migrate/` now
  has the full shared shape (`driver` · `history` · `migrator` · `ddl`);
  `@sisal/mysql/migrate` is exported. Pinned by 7 network-free unit tests;
  verified live on **both engines × both drivers** (four combinations): migrate
  → idempotent re-run → rollback with B5-**generated** DDL as the migration
  bodies, ledger round-trip (`appliedAt` matched true UTC against the wall clock
  — the Date-param local-serialize/local-parse pair cancels symmetrically), and
  real two-connection lock contention (`A=true B=false` → release → `B=true`).
  - **`GET_LOCK`/`RELEASE_LOCK` named locks** — the `pg_advisory_lock` analogue
    the C4 report called for. Names pass through verbatim (no hashing, unlike
    pg's FNV-hashed bigint keys), validated non-empty and ≤ 64 chars (the MySQL
    cap) before any session is acquired. `GET_LOCK` is **connection-scoped**, so
    the store holds it on a pinned executor session (`acquireSession`, the pg
    design) — the lock dies with the connection, which is the crash-safety
    property advisory locking wants. The store coerces the lock result robustly:
    the mandated bigint-as-string driver options decode `GET_LOCK`'s BIGINT as
    the _string_ `"1"` on mysql2 (number on the mariadb connector) — both
    pinned.
  - **The history ledger obeys the adapter's own B5 rules:**
    `id varchar(255) primary key` (a `TEXT` key is invalid MySQL) and
    `applied_at datetime(6)` (no timezone conversion, no 2038 cliff);
    `appliedAt` is written as a `Date` param because an ISO string's trailing
    `Z` is rejected by `DATETIME`.
  - **`useTransaction` defaults to `false`** — unlike pg: MySQL/MariaDB have no
    transactional DDL (every `CREATE`/`ALTER` implicitly commits), so a wrap
    around schema migrations is a false promise. Opt-in remains for DML-only
    migrations, where it also makes the history mark atomic.
  - The migrate executor reuses the ORM adapter's connection-source routing, so
    `driver: "mariadb"` (and its B4 Buffer fix + decode options) applies to
    migrations for free.
- **B7 — `RETURNING` execution strategy. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** The new
  `insertReturning(db, table, values)` helper
  (`packages/mysql/orm/returning.ts`), pinned by 8 network-free unit tests and
  verified live on **both engines × both drivers**. Strategy selection is
  automatic and rows come back in input order under both:
  - **Attempt real `INSERT … RETURNING` first** — on a facade whose detected
    identity lights it (MariaDB ≥ 10.5) this is one statement with true
    semantics. The helper catches exactly the core guard's typed error
    (`ORM_DIALECT_UNSUPPORTED` + `details.construct`) and falls back — the core
    stays the **single source of truth** for variant/version floors; no
    duplicated version logic in the adapter.
  - **Fetch-by-key fallback** in a transaction: all-explicit primary keys → one
    `INSERT` + one `SELECT` back; a single-column key with missing values →
    **one `INSERT` per row capturing each statement's own `LAST_INSERT_ID`**.
    Deliberately no first-id-plus-offset arithmetic: MySQL 8.4's live-confirmed
    default `innodb_autoinc_lock_mode = 2` (interleaved; MariaDB 11.8 ships `1`)
    does not guarantee a batch's ids are consecutive, so the arithmetic shortcut
    (Drizzle's `$returningId` approach) can silently return someone else's rows.
  - **Honest corners fail typed** (`ORM_INVALID_QUERY`), never wrong: no primary
    key, a composite key not fully provided, a `Sql`-expression key value, or a
    server-generated non-`AUTO_INCREMENT` key (`DEFAULT (uuid())` is invisible
    to `LAST_INSERT_ID`) — the live probe shows the same call returning real
    rows on MariaDB (`RETURNING` sees the generated uuid) and refusing typed on
    MySQL.
  - Plumbing: `insertId` now flows executor → driver → facade result
    (`MysqlQueryResult.insertId`; 0 normalized to absent; the mariadb
    connector's `bigint` re-viewed as a precision-safe string), and key matching
    stringifies both sides — the same id can surface as number 7
    (`LAST_INSERT_ID`), string `"7"` (bigint-as-string column decode), or
    `bigint` depending on driver and column type.
- **B8 — the integration proof. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** The reserved `"mysql"`
  slot is filled and the fifth/sixth feature-matrix columns are live and
  test-backed. **42 shared mysql-family scenarios** run green against **both**
  engines (`integration/mysql_features_test.ts` on MySQL 8.4.10,
  `integration/mariadb_features_test.ts` on MariaDB 11.8.8), and also pass on
  the opt-in mariadb driver:
  - **One scenario list, two capability profiles.** MySQL and MariaDB are one
    adapter but distinct profiles (`IntegrationAdapterId` gained `"mariadb"`);
    `makeMysqlFamilyTarget` builds both from a shared factory that differs only
    in `returning`, `json`/`array` value shape, and env gate. Scenarios branch
    on the target's declared capabilities, so each divergence is a **tested
    fact**: MySQL's `.returning()` throws the typed guard (and the B7
    `insertReturning` fetch-by-key strategy carries the insert scenario), while
    MariaDB's auto-detected identity lights `INSERT`/`DELETE … RETURNING`;
    `UPDATE … RETURNING`, `FULL JOIN`, `distinctOn`, dm-CTEs, and
    partial/expression indexes all assert their typed guards live. B10 later
    moves `UPDATE … FROM`/`DELETE … USING` from that guard set into tested MySQL
    multi-table mutation support.
  - **Matrix and docs.** `tools/feature_matrix.ts` gained `mysql`+`mariadb`
    columns (36 features × 6 adapters = 216 cells; 198 ✅/⚠️ each backed by a
    named scenario, enforced by `docs:matrix:check`); MariaDB is its own column,
    not a footnote (`RETURNING` and JSON-as-text prove it).
    `docs/mysql-compatibility.md` is the per-engine page, and the homepage
    `#compat` section gained MySQL/MariaDB badges + link.
  - **Three real bugs the live suite caught** (invisible to the network-free
    unit tests, all fixed in core/adapter and re-pinned): JSON/array params
    reached mysql2's text protocol un-serialized (an object became invalid SQL,
    an array **expanded** into N params, silently shifting later placeholders) —
    the executor now `JSON.stringify`s plain objects/arrays; `DATETIME` columns
    decoded to client-local `Date`s (timezone-shifting server values) — the pool
    now sets `dateStrings: true`; and instants serialized with a trailing `Z`
    that MySQL rejects in datetime literals — the core renderer now tags instant
    params and rewrites them to **naive UTC** under the `mysql` render dialect
    (the "executor UTC convention"), leaving the `postgres`, `sqlite`, and
    `generic` render paths untouched.
- **B9 — CLI target + basic example. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).**
  `sisal init --target mysql` now scaffolds a `dialect: "mysql"` migration
  config with `MYSQL_URL ?? DATABASE_URL` connection hints; `mariadb` is an
  alias for the same adapter target. The CLI default adapter loader resolves
  `@sisal/mysql/ddl` and `@sisal/mysql/migrate` for `generate`, `migrate`,
  `status`, and `drift`. `examples/mysql-family-basic` joins the runnable
  workspace examples: it prints MySQL DDL without a database, and runs a tiny
  connect/create/insert/count flow against `mysql2` or the MariaDB connector via
  `SISAL_ADAPTER`.
- **B10 — multi-table mutation mapping. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** The existing
  portable builders are unchanged, but the mysql renderer now maps
  `update(t).from(source)` to `UPDATE t, source SET t.col = … WHERE …` and
  `delete(t).using(source)` to `DELETE FROM t USING t, source WHERE …`.
  PostgreSQL/SQLite `UPDATE … FROM` rendering is unchanged; SQLite
  `DELETE … USING` remains a typed guard. Multi-table mutation plus
  `.returning()` remains guarded for the MySQL family, including MariaDB,
  because the proven B7 `RETURNING` support is per-statement/single-table. The
  shared mysql-family integration scenario now exercises `UPDATE FROM`,
  `DELETE USING`, and `INSERT SELECT`, and the matrix marks the MySQL/MariaDB
  mutation-joins cells as ✅.
  - **Follow-up (2026-07-02, CI-caught):** MariaDB parses a `WITH` prefix only
    on `SELECT`, so the CTE-prefixed forms of these mutations were a raw 1064
    there. The `WITH` prefix on `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` is now a
    variant-narrowed typed guard (`{ dialect: "mysql", variant: "mariadb" }`),
    the scenario branches on a `mutationCte` capability (MariaDB asserts the
    guard and runs the same mutation via a derived-table source), and the
    divergence is a row in [mysql-compatibility.md](mysql-compatibility.md).
    Mutation joins themselves stay ✅ on both engines.

## Deliverables

`@sisal/mysql` published to **JSR** (npm deferred — see the
[npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md)), wired into the
workspace, `check` task, CLI, CI matrix, and the feature matrix.

## Non-goals

MySQL does **not** become the ETL/analytics reference (Postgres stays the
reference); no MySQL-specific ETL/analytics features beyond dialect parity; full
MariaDB parity only if the v0.6 investigation scoped it in.

## Risks

- ~~**Upsert / `RETURNING` divergence** — the most likely source of a leaky
  abstraction.~~ **Retired by v0.6 C2/C3:** the mapping is implemented and
  pinned (upsert renders correct MySQL; `RETURNING` throws a typed guard); what
  remains is B7's execution-side strategy, a bounded design.
- ~~**Driver maturity on Deno**~~ — **retired by v0.6 C6:** both drivers were
  benchmarked from Deno 2.9 against real MySQL 8.4.10 and MariaDB 11.8.8 and
  dual-runtime-verified on Node 26; no Nagle-class stall exists in any
  candidate.
- **Type round-trip surprises** — mostly de-risked by the C4 probe
  (`TINYINT(1)`, `DATETIME(6)`, JSON-as-text on MariaDB, the lossy-bigint
  default are all known and specced), but the executor implementation can still
  get them wrong — the value-shape scenario assertions in B8 are the guard.
- **Test infrastructure** — Docker `mysql:8.4` + `mariadb:11` services in CI,
  gated like the existing suites (the C-series probes already run against
  exactly these images locally).
- ~~**Sequencing** — B1 (the dialect axis) must land before the v0.8 IR freeze,
  and B2 gates B8 (the shared scenario bodies use `filter()`/date helpers).~~
  **Retired (2026-07-01):** B1 and B2 both landed first, in order — the freeze
  deadline is met and B8 is unblocked.

## Open questions

- MySQL 8 only, or MySQL + MariaDB (affects `RETURNING`, sequences, JSON funcs)?
  **Resolved by v0.6 C5:** one adapter, **both** — MySQL ≥ 8.0.16 baseline,
  MariaDB ≥ 10.10 via the variant-aware capability descriptor; the dialect key
  is `(engine, variant, version)`, so MariaDB / older-MySQL handling stays
  **additive**.
- ~~Is `onDuplicateKeyUpdate` a MySQL-only builder method, or does `onConflict*`
  dialect-map onto it?~~ **Answered by v0.6 C2:** `onConflict*` dialect-maps (no
  separate surface), with the typed `excluded()` helper.
- ~~Does the latent `"mysql"` row-value/keyset path actually work, or need
  fixes?~~ **Answered by B8 (2026-07-01): it works.** Both keyset forms —
  expanded and row-value `(a, b) < (x, y)` — paginate identically live on MySQL
  8.4 and MariaDB 11.8 (the "keyset pagination" scenario runs both forms and
  asserts the same id order).
- ~~Where does the `TINYINT(1)` → `boolean` decode decision land (executor
  default vs opt-in), given the SQLite family's `0`/`1` precedent?~~ **Decided
  (B4, 2026-07-01): no auto-decode** — `BOOLEAN` round-trips as `0`/`1`,
  matching the SQLite family's documented value shape. A `TINYINT(1)` display
  width does not guarantee boolean semantics (users store 0–127), so
  auto-decoding would corrupt legitimate tiny integers; the matrix marks it ⚠
  like sqlite/libsql. Revisit only if a reliable signal appears.

## Acceptance criteria

- `@sisal/mysql` passes a mysql-family scenario suite covering the shared OLTP
  surface (CRUD, joins incl. the `FULL JOIN` guard, tx, prepared, `SELECT` CTEs,
  upsert via the C2 mapping, `filter()`/date helpers once B2 lifts their guards,
  migrations) against `mysql:8.4` **and** `mariadb:11`, gated by
  `SISAL_MYSQL_IT=1`.
- The feature matrix gains **test-backed MySQL (and, where they diverge,
  MariaDB) columns**; divergences are `⚠️` with documented round-trip notes,
  genuine limits are `❌`. (MariaDB is a distinct capability profile, not a
  footnote on one "fifth column" — `RETURNING` alone proves it.)
- Upsert and `RETURNING` behave correctly or fail/emulate as documented.
- The published dialect identity carries a **variant/version tag** (`mysql` vs
  `mariadb`, server version); any capability that differs by variant/version
  (`RETURNING`, `WITH RECURSIVE`, `JSON_TABLE`) is gated on it and fails
  **typed**, not as a raw engine error. This is the adapter-level seed of the
  v0.8 capability descriptor.
- `@sisal/orm`/core render output for the other four adapters (pg, neon, sqlite,
  libsql) is unchanged.

## Affected packages

**New `@sisal/mysql`**; `@sisal/orm`/core (latent `"mysql"` render corrections +
`dialectGuard` set); `@sisal/migrate` (CLI `INIT_TARGETS` entry, MySQL DDL);
`tools/feature_matrix.ts` + `docs/mysql-compatibility.md` + the unified matrix;
`.github/workflows` (MySQL service).

## Required examples / tests

- A gated `integration/mysql_features_test.ts` entrypoint filling the reserved
  `"mysql"` slot of the consolidated integration structure (an
  `IntegrationTarget` in `integration/_targets/` + a `mysql_family_scenarios.ts`
  in `integration/_shared/`).
- Network-free render tests for the MySQL dialect — **the v0.6 baseline already
  exists** (`packages/orm/mysql_dialect_test.ts`, `etl_rollup_test.ts`); extend
  rather than start.
- MySQL-family runnable examples following the dialect-family taxonomy:
  `examples/mysql-family-basic`, `examples/mysql-family-showcase`, and
  `examples/mysql-family-feed`. The feed is intentionally product-shaped, not a
  thin smoke test: it uses MySQL-safe `DATETIME(6)` literals, `varchar(36)`
  keys, row-value keyset pagination, affected-row actor dedupe, and both
  TypeScript and builder-CTE rising-score recompute paths.

## Dialect compatibility notes

MySQL 8 supports CTEs, window functions, and row locking (much of the surface is
portable — the v0.6 C5 probe verified windows/CTEs/`INTERSECT`/`EXCEPT` on both
engines). The upsert divergence is **already mapped** (C2); the remaining
principled divergences are **uneven `RETURNING`** (guard today, B7 strategy),
**`BOOLEAN`=`TINYINT(1)`**, **JSON decoding** (parsed on MySQL, text on
MariaDB), and **MySQL-vs-MariaDB** (`LATERAL`/`->>`/functional indexes
MySQL-only; `SEQUENCE`/native `UUID`/extended `TIMESTAMP` MariaDB-only — carried
by the B1 capability descriptor). `distinctOn`, the Postgres array operators,
`FULL JOIN`, and data-modifying CTEs stay `❌` (guarded). This column feeds the
v0.9 hardening and the ETL/analytics dialect matrices.

---

## Tracking (both workstreams)

When an item lands: implement it, add coverage (network-free render/type tests
for A; gated integration tests for B), update the feature matrix, flip status to
🟢, and record under `Unreleased` in [CHANGELOG.md](../CHANGELOG.md). v0.7 is
complete when the analytics-readiness report + IR sketch exist **and**
`@sisal/mysql` ships at documented OLTP parity with a test-backed matrix column.
```

### docs/v0.8.0-roadmap.md

URL: https://gilvandovieira.github.io/sisal/v0.8.0-roadmap.html

```md
---
title: v0.8.0 Roadmap
---

# Sisal v0.8.0 roadmap — Advanced SQL IR & Expression Stabilization

v0.6 and v0.7 are investigations; v0.8 is where their findings become a **stable
substrate**. The goal is to stabilize Sisal's internal SQL representation and
expression system — and extract **`@sisal/core`** — so that the future
`@sisal/etl` and `@sisal/analytics` packages can **compile into Sisal safely**,
without forcing invasive ORM rewrites later.

This release ships the first shared **core** package boundary —
**`@sisal/core`** — the substrate every other package builds on (v0.7 already
added an adapter package, `@sisal/mysql`).

## Status legend

| Symbol | Meaning                                                    |
| ------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| 🔴     | Not started · 🟡 In progress · 🟢 Done — move to CHANGELOG |

## Priority summary — everything to track

All v0.8 tasks, consolidated from this document's scope/deliverables, the
v0.5/v0.6/v0.7 handoffs, and the
[sequencing audit](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md)'s v0.8 gates. Each item points
at the section of this document (or the audit fix) that specifies it.

| #  | Item                                                                                      | Priority | Rough effort | Status |
| -- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------- | ------------ | ------ |
| 1  | Capability descriptor: pin the `(engine, variant, version-range)` key space (audit Fix 1) | P1       | L            | 🟢     |
| 2  | Extract `@sisal/core` (file-moves + barrel split; re-point orm/migrate/adapters; JSR)     | P1       | L            | 🟢     |
| 3  | Version + document the public IR/compile-target surface (`Sql`/`SqlChunk` contract)       | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 4  | Transformable-AST decision **+ ship the additive extension point** (audit Fix 3)          | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 5  | Statement-assembly seam decision — expose assembly API vs fragments-only (audit Fix 4)    | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 6  | `@sisal/migrate` dependency decision (core-only vs orm) + import-migration notes          | P2       | S            | 🟢     |
| 7  | First-class typed expression aliases (name-once metric/projection expressions)            | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 8  | Computed columns + nested selects — typed, reusable, inference-checked                    | P2       | L            | 🟢     |
| 9  | Typed `coalesce`/`greatest` (retire the last raw seams in the composed rollups)           | P2       | S            | 🟢     |
| 10 | Core `over()` + window/frame/ranking/`lag`/`lead` + portable date-diff/gap helpers        | P2       | L            | 🟢     |
| 11 | Recursive CTE builder (`WITH RECURSIVE`, depth/cycle guards, version-aware gates)         | P2       | M            | 🟢     |
| 12 | Golden per-dialect SQL suites + parameter-binding + prepared-plan round-trips             | P1       | L            | 🟢     |
| 13 | `@sisal/core`-only fixture: compile the `post_hourly_stats` rollup via core exports       | P1       | M            | 🟢     |
| 14 | `ARRAY[…]` / `unnest` / JSON-table set-returning IR with typed projected columns          | P3       | L            | 🟢     |
| 15 | Generated columns + expression/partial indexes in snapshots/DDL, typed unsupported errors | P3       | L            | 🟢     |
| 16 | Write-result abstraction decision (`inserted` vs `conflicted`/`claimed` result shape)     | P3       | S            | 🟢     |
| 17 | Portability policy docs: UTC literals, `dateTrunc`/`dateBin` shapes, DDL-cleanup pattern  | P3       | S            | 🟢     |
| 18 | Checkpoint-table ownership decision — etl-managed vs migrate system table (audit Fix 2)   | P2       | S            | 🟢     |
| 19 | Upsert assignment abstraction — make ODKU left-to-right evaluation order safe/explicit    | P3       | S            | 🟢     |

**P1 spine.** Item 1 first — the audit's 🟥 gate (GI-1/G6): the descriptor's key
space must be settled **at v0.8, before the IR freezes**, because the exported
guard/IR surface cannot migrate afterwards (v0.7's B1
`(engine, variant, version)` identity is the adapter-level seed it generalizes).
Then 2/3 (the extraction and what freezes with it) with 4/5 settled **in the
same release** — the audit's other two gates: the AST seam (item 4) keeps v0.13
DuckDB pushdown unblocked without building the AST now, and the assembler
decision (item 5) is the dependency-direction gate that decides whether
v0.10/v0.11 can stay off `@sisal/orm`. Items 7 and 12/13 are the proof
obligations: aliases feed every downstream metric column, and the golden suites
plus the core-only rollup fixture are what "stable compile target" means in
practice. Items 8–11 are the expression-surface work the
[example graduations](#findings-from-advanced-sql-example-graduation) demand
(over()/windows per the [A3 seam](analytics-readiness.md); recursive CTEs per
contract 07); 14–17 and 19 are electives that can slip to v0.9+ without breaking
a gate.

**Routed elsewhere (tracked here so they aren't lost):** the portable
queue-claim/advisory-lock abstraction (contract 08) and the checkpoint/watermark
contracts (contract 09) belong to **v0.9** per the master sequencing (v0.6
documents → v0.9 implements → v0.10 consumes) — and per
[audit Fix 2](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md) they must become **v0.9 acceptance
criteria**, not droppable hardening items; v0.8 only supplies the SQL-expression
substrate they ride on, plus the ownership _decision_ (item 18, which — if it
resolves to "migrate-managed" — adds an `etl → migrate` edge that
`architecture.md`'s graph must then show). Window-function _rendering_ lands
here (item 10), but the rich metric/dimension API stays in v0.11's
`@sisal/analytics` per the v0.7 A3 decision.

Item sources: 1 — [acceptance criteria](#acceptance-criteria) +
[audit Fix 1](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md); 2/3/6 — [scope](#scope--focus) +
[deliverables](#deliverables); 4 — [open questions](#open-questions) +
[audit Fix 3](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md); 5 — [deliverables](#deliverables) +
[audit Fix 4](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md); 7/8/9 — scope +
[MySQL-family findings](#findings-from-mysql-family-examples) (expression-reuse
finding: raw `coalesce`/`greatest`/rising-score seams) + v0.6 A1's `coalesce`
candidate; 10/11/14/15 —
[graduation table](#findings-from-advanced-sql-example-graduation) contracts
02/03/04/05 (03/05 add the date-diff/gap helpers), 07, 10, 11; 12/13 —
[required examples / tests](#required-examples--tests); 16/17/19 — the
MySQL-family findings (write-result shape, actor dedupe, time handling, the
non-transactional-DDL cleanup pattern, the ODKU assignment-order hazard) and the
cross-cutting pain points in both findings sections; 18 —
[audit Fix 2](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md) ("resolve checkpoint-table ownership
in v0.8/v0.9, before v0.10 ships the table").

## Execution order

The item numbers above are stable tracking IDs, **not** a build sequence — read
literally they stamp the surface before the seams that change it and refactor
the guard machinery without a net. The dependency-compatible order is five
waves; items within a wave can run in parallel.

**Wave 0 — the net (first half of item 12). ✅ Done (2026-07-02).** Golden
per-dialect SQL baselines for every _existing_ IR construct, before anything
moves. Wave 1 rewrites the guard machinery and wave 2 relocates every core file;
the baselines are what prove both are behavior-preserving (the same
pin-then-change discipline the v0.7 B-stream used). Shipped as
[`packages/orm/golden_sql_test.ts`](../packages/orm/golden_sql_test.ts): 49
constructs × 5 render targets (postgres/sqlite/mysql/generic + the detected
MariaDB 11.8.8 identity) snapshotted with text, ordered params, and typed guard
errors — including the identity-divergent baselines (MariaDB `RETURNING` lift,
`WITH`-on-mutation guards, the instant naive-UTC rewrite) — plus per-dialect
prepared-plan placeholder styles. Update intentionally via
`deno test --allow-read --allow-write packages/orm/golden_sql_test.ts -- --update`.
The remaining half of 12 — goldens for _new_ constructs — accrues per item in
wave 3, not as a phase of its own.

**Wave 1 — decisions that shape what freezes: 1, 4, 5, 6+18, 16 (decision only).
✅ Done (2026-07-02).** Landed under the wave-0 net (every golden snapshot
byte-identical): **item 1** — the capability registry
(`packages/core/capabilities.ts` after extraction): `DIALECT_CAPABILITIES`
declares every render-guarded construct's `(engine, variant, version-range)`
truth as the same serializable data the `guard` chunk carries; every guard call
site now derives its render guard from the registry via `capabilityGuard`, and
`capabilitySupported` answers the same question without rendering (identical
fail-closed semantics through the shared `dialectGuardApplies`) —
`CAPABILITY_TARGETS` names the six matrix targets as `(engine, variant)`
identities; pinned by `capability_test.ts` incl. the registry/renderer agreement
invariant. Wave 5 adds the matrix-target wiring that closes item 1; per-cell
matrix value derivation remains v0.9. **Item 4** — the `SqlChunk` `meta` seam is
shipped and fixture-proven (see open questions; 🟢). **Items 5/6/18/16** —
decisions closed and recorded in [open questions](#open-questions): minimal core
assembly API (5; fixture proof lands in wave 4); `@sisal/migrate` → core only
(6; notes land in wave 2); checkpoints stay etl-managed, no `etl → migrate` edge
(18); and the write-result abstraction (16 as a decision): record the direction
— a portable write-outcome ("inserted" vs "conflicted"/"claimed") surfaced per
adapter — and **defer implementation** to ride with v0.9's queue-claim/lock
work; until then the feature-matrix round-trip notes stay authoritative. Item 1
is the long pole (the 🟥 gate) and rewrites `dialectGuard` under the wave-0 net.
Item 4's seam is a small `SqlChunk` change. Items 5 and 16 are decisions whose
outcomes are part of the stamped surface. Items 6 and 18 are coupled — both
decide `migrate`-edge topology — so decide them together. Everything in this
wave changes _what_ item 2 moves and item 3 stamps, which is why both wait.

**Wave 2 — the move: 2. ✅ Done (2026-07-02).** Extraction after the shapes are
final, so files move exactly once and the re-pointing churn across
orm/migrate/adapters happens in one pass (with item 6's migration notes). Before
the construct work, so waves 3–4 land directly in `@sisal/core` instead of being
moved after the fact. Shipped: `packages/core/` (`@sisal/core@0.8.0`, exports
`.`, `./schema`, `./unstable-internal`) holds the lower tier (`error`, `logger`,
`schema`, `errors`, `sql`, `capabilities`, `operators`, `columns`, `temporal`,
`table`); the ORM tier (`builders`, `database`, `functions`, `relations`) stays
in `packages/orm/core/` and reaches non-public plumbing only through the
`unstable-internal` seam. `@sisal/orm` re-exports everything (its `/core`,
`/schema`, `/error`, `/logger` subpaths are compatibility re-exports — no
user-visible change, proven by the byte-identical golden baselines and full
suite); `@sisal/migrate` now imports `@sisal/core` only, closing item 6 with
[core-migration.md](core-migration.md) as the notes. JSR publish dry-run passes
for all eight packages. Item 3 (versioning + documenting the public surface)
deliberately stays open for wave 4.

**Wave 3 — expression surface, in the new package: 7 → 9, then 8, 10, 11. ✅
Done (2026-07-02).** Item 7 first — aliases are what 8/13 reuse and the cheapest
surface change. Items 10, 11, 14, and 15 _consume_ item 1's version-aware gates,
which is why none of them can close before wave 1. Shipped, each construct with
its golden coverage (contributing to item 12) and **every semantic verified live
on PostgreSQL 16, MySQL 8.4, MariaDB 11.8, and embedded SQLite with identical
values** before pinning:

- **7** — `expr<T>()` types a fragment without the old `as SqlExpression<T>`
  casts; expression handles were already accepted uniformly (projection,
  `groupBy`, `having`, `orderBy`, operators funnel through `columnToSql`), so
  name-once reuse is a const + re-render, pinned in the expression tests. Item 8
  moves to 🟡: computed-expression projection inference and nested selects
  (subqueries/`exists`) pre-existed and are now pinned via item 7 — the
  remaining 8-scope (reusable computed-column declarations on tables) is
  re-derived in wave 4 or slips to v0.9 without breaking a gate.
- **9** — typed `coalesce`, plus `greatest`/`least` (SQLite renders scalar
  `max`/`min`; per-engine NULL divergence documented — wrap operands in
  `coalesce`).
- **10** — `core/window.ts`: `over()` with `WindowSpec` (partition/order/frame
  per the analytics-readiness signature),
  `rank`/`denseRank`/`rowNumber`/`lag`/`lead`, and `dateDiff` (truncated whole
  units: `TIMESTAMPDIFF` on the MySQL family, epoch-`trunc` on PostgreSQL,
  `julianday` on SQLite). Two registry capabilities landed with it: `GROUPS`
  frames (MySQL family never; SQLite version-gated ≥ 3.28 via the new
  **dialect-scoped guard exception** — an item-1 key-space extension this
  construct forced) and the `lag()`/`lead()` **default argument**, which the
  live probe caught MariaDB 11.8 rejecting outright (MySQL 8.4 accepts it) —
  guarded typed, portable spelling `coalesce(over(lag(x), …), d)`.
- **11** — `db.$withRecursive(name, columns).as((self) => base.unionAll(step))`
  renders `WITH RECURSIVE name (cols) AS (…)` with the self-reference usable as
  a source, one `RECURSIVE` keyword covering mixed lists, and the portable
  depth-guard pattern (`WHERE depth < n`) documented and live-verified;
  PostgreSQL 14's `CYCLE` clause is deliberately not rendered (future,
  version-gated).

**Wave 4 — proof and stamp: 13, then 3, then 17. ✅ Done (2026-07-02).** The
core-only rollup fixture (13) needs item 5's decision (what a downstream package
may use) and exercises 7/9; it is the acceptance-criteria proof that the compile
target is real. Item 3 — versioning and documenting the public surface — goes
**last** deliberately: it stamps the net result of 1/4/5/7/16, and stamping
earlier means re-versioning after every wave-3 addition. Shipped:

- **5+13** — `@sisal/core`'s `assemble.ts` (`assembleSelect`,
  `assembleInsertFromSelect` with the dialect-mapped upsert) implements the
  item-5 decision; `packages/core/rollup_fixture_test.ts` compiles the canonical
  `post_hourly_stats` rollup **using only core exports** and pins the exact SQL;
  `packages/orm/assembly_equivalence_test.ts` proves the assembly and the fluent
  builder render **byte-identical text and params on every dialect** — the two
  surfaces cannot drift.
- **3** — `SQL_IR_VERSION = 1` exported from core, and [core-ir.md](core-ir.md)
  documents the compile-target contract: the three export surfaces and their
  commitments, the chunk-kind table with render semantics, the compatibility
  policy (additive = minor; the `meta` seam is the reserved AST path; the golden
  suites are the behavioral pin), and what is deliberately not promised.
- **17** — [portability-policies.md](portability-policies.md): the UTC literal
  policy (the "executor UTC convention"), the `dateTrunc`/`dateBin`/`dateDiff`
  value-shape table with the compare-within-one-engine rule, and the DDL-cleanup
  pattern (no transactional DDL on the MySQL family).
- **8 closed alongside**: computed expressions (`expr` + projection inference)
  and nested selects (scalar subqueries in projections, now golden-pinned) plus
  the assembly seam cover its scope; table-level generated columns are tracked
  separately as item 15 and close in wave 5.
- The golden suite grew to **59 snapshots** (scalar-subquery projection + the
  assembly statement), all prior keys byte-identical, and
  `benchmarks/scenarios/advanced_sql.ts` gives the wave-3/4 constructs
  render-throughput baselines for future refactors.

**Wave 5 — the remaining items closed (2026-07-02).** The last four items (1,
14, 15, 19), all verified live on PostgreSQL 16, MySQL 8.4, MariaDB 11.8, and
embedded SQLite:

- **19 — ODKU assignment order.** The renderer preserves `onConflictDoUpdate`
  set order verbatim, and injects a MySQL-only typed guard when an assignment
  reads a _different_ sibling column set **earlier** in the same list (the
  left-to-right footgun) — self-references, forward references (derived-first),
  and `excluded()` pass. Live: derived-first computes the pre-update value on
  every engine; the buggy order throws typed. Policy documented in
  [portability-policies.md](portability-policies.md); pinned by
  `odku_assignment_order_test.ts`.
- **15 — generated columns.** `.generatedAs(sql, { stored })` on the column
  builder flows into the snapshot (`generatedAs`, portable expression text,
  change-detected in the diff) and emits
  `GENERATED ALWAYS AS (…)
  STORED|VIRTUAL` in the pg/sqlite/mysql DDL
  generators — VIRTUAL fails closed typed on PostgreSQL, and a generated column
  can't also carry a default. (Expression + partial indexes were already
  snapshot/DDL-native; only generated columns remained.) Live: the generated
  column computes on all four engines.
- **14 — array / JSON / set-returning IR.** `arrayExpr` (`ARRAY[…]` /
  `json_array`), `jsonExtract` (portable scalar path extraction), and
  `jsonTable` — the set-returning source compiling to PostgreSQL
  `jsonb_to_recordset`, MySQL `JSON_TABLE`, and SQLite `json_each` + per-field
  `json_extract`, with typed column refs and a raw-`Sql` `.from()` overload
  (also composes with `assembleSelect`). Live: `jsonTable` explodes a JSON array
  into typed rows identically on all four engines. Golden entries added (62
  snapshots).
- **1 — capability descriptor.** Already the single source `dialectGuard` reads;
  now the feature-matrix generator consumes the registry's `CAPABILITY_TARGETS`
  and `docs:matrix:check` fails if the matrix's `ADAPTERS` and the registry's
  six-way target space diverge — closing the GI-1 key-space-reconciliation
  concern. The per-cell capability-value wiring (matrix cells derived from
  `capabilitySupported`) remains a **v0.9** acceptance criterion, as its roadmap
  already states.

All 19 v0.8 items are 🟢. The only v0.8-adjacent work deferred to v0.9 is the
per-cell matrix-value wiring (item 1's completion) and the follow-ups logged
during the advanced-SQL review (MySQL functional-index version gate, example
refresh, recursive-CTE `CYCLE`).

Two edges worth restating because they are easy to violate: **3 concludes, it
does not lead** (its P1 rank reflects importance, not position), and **nothing
version-gated (10/11/14/15) finishes before 1** — a construct shipped against
the old 4-value guard would need immediate rework.

## Goal

Turn today's two-tier representation into a documented, versioned, public
**compile target**: a stable fragment IR + expression system + dialect interface
that downstream packages build on, with golden per-dialect SQL tests guarding
it.

## What grounds this (audit)

Sisal already has a clean internal split: a **dialect-agnostic fragment IR**
(`Sql`/`SqlChunk`, `Condition`, `SqlExpression`, `OrderTerm`) rendered by a
single two-phase renderer, plus a **module-private** statement-level state
machine (`SelectState` + the clause assembler). The lower tier (`errors`, `sql`,
`operators`, `columns`, `table`, `temporal`, zero-import `schema.ts`) has **no
upward edges** into the OLTP builders, and the one cross-cut
(builder-as-subquery) is already inverted behind `QUERY_BUILDER_BRAND` +
`SubquerySource`. So extraction is **file-moves + barrel-split + a new
`deno.json`**, not a rewrite.

The key limitation v0.8 must consciously address: the fragment IR is
**compose-oriented, not transform-oriented** — expressions are stored
already-lowered (`eq()` flattens immediately); there is **no introspectable,
rewritable relational AST**.

## Scope / focus

- **Extract `@sisal/core`** — move the lower tier into a `@sisal/core` package
  (schema primitives, fragment IR, expressions/operators, dialect interface +
  renderer); re-point `@sisal/orm`, `@sisal/migrate`, and the adapters at it.
- **Stable SQL IR/AST boundaries** — version the `Sql`/`SqlChunk` contract;
  decide and document what is public (compile target) vs internal.
- **Expression aliasing** — first-class typed aliases for projection/metric
  columns (needed by ETL rollup columns and analytics metrics).
- **Computed columns** and **nested selects** — typed, reusable.
- **CTE rendering** — including the path to **data-modifying CTEs**
  (`WITH … INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE … RETURNING`, v0.5 item 12) and
  **insert-from-select rendering** (the v0.6 gap).
- **Aggregate expression rendering**; **window function rendering** where
  supported (the v0.7 prerequisite).
- **Dialect capability gates** — generalize the v0.5 `dialectGuard` into the
  explicit capability surface v0.9 hardens.
- **Golden SQL tests per dialect**; **parameter-binding correctness**;
  **prepared-plan compatibility**; **type inference for computed expressions**.

## Deliverables

- `@sisal/core` package with a documented public IR/compile-target surface.
- A statement-assembly seam: decide whether to **expose** a minimal builder-
  assembly API (so ETL/analytics reuse SELECT/CTE assembly) or keep it private
  and have downstream packages compile fragments directly. This decides whether
  ETL/analytics can stay off `@sisal/orm` (the dependency-direction gate), so it
  must be settled in v0.8, before v0.10/v0.11 consume core. (See open
  questions.)
- Golden-SQL snapshot tests per dialect for every IR construct.
- Migration notes for the `@sisal/orm` → `@sisal/core` import changes
  (`@sisal/orm/core` stays as a compatibility re-export; the notes cover when to
  prefer importing `@sisal/core` directly).

## Non-goals

No ETL/analytics package (v0.10/v0.11); no DuckDB; no query optimizer/planner;
no breaking change to the public ORM builder API — including import paths:
`@sisal/orm/core` **remains as a compatibility re-export** of `@sisal/core`, so
existing imports keep working; new code should import `@sisal/core` directly.

## Risks

- **The "do we need a transformable AST?" fork** — predicate pushdown / plan
  rewriting (relevant to DuckDB pushdown, v0.13+) would be **new core work** the
  current fragment IR cannot do. Deciding this wrong over- or under-builds v0.8.
- **Public IR = compatibility commitment** — once `@sisal/core`'s IR is public,
  it's a contract; under-specifying it forces a later breaking change.
- **Extraction churn** — touching import paths across every package; mitigated
  by the clean DAG but still broad.
- **Window/data-modifying-CTE rendering divergence** across dialects.

## Findings from MySQL-family examples

Building `examples/mysql-family-showcase` and `examples/mysql-family-feed` made
these v0.8 pressure points concrete:

- **DDL rollback is not portable.** The PostgreSQL showcase can run inside a
  rollback transaction; MySQL/MariaDB DDL implicitly commits, so the MySQL
  showcase must create namespaced tables and drop them in cleanup.
- **Write-result shape matters.** MySQL proper lacks general `.returning()`, so
  flows need `insertReturning` or fetch-after-write. MariaDB lights only some
  `RETURNING` forms by variant/version, proving the capability descriptor must
  be declarative and version-aware.
- **Actor dedupe exposes a missing abstraction.** The feed detects first touch
  in a bucket by pre-checking the actor row and then reading
  `onConflictDoNothing().execute().rowCount` on the insert path; rowCount alone
  was not reliable with the portable MySQL no-op update rendering. Sisal has no
  higher-level "inserted vs conflicted" result, so application code must know
  affected-row semantics.
- **MySQL upsert assignment order leaks through.** The bucket upsert must assign
  the derived `activity_score` before incrementing the counters, because MySQL
  evaluates `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` assignments left-to-right. A future
  expression/assignment abstraction should make that hazard harder to miss.
- **Expression reuse is still awkward.** The builder-native CTE recompute
  repeats filtered aggregate fragments and derived metric expressions; it also
  uses raw `sql` for `coalesce`, `greatest`, and the final rising-score formula.
  This strengthens the case for expression aliases and computed fields in the
  public compile target.
- **Statement assembly is a real boundary.** The feed wants a stable
  statement-assembly or `@sisal/core` compile target for chained SELECT CTEs,
  multi-table update, MariaDB's CTE-update fallback, and fetch-after-write
  orchestration, but today that composition depends on ORM builders.
- **Time handling must be explicit.** MySQL `DATETIME(6)` paths need a
  documented UTC literal policy (`YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss.SSS000`), and helpers such
  as `dateTrunc`/`dateBin` return string-like values on MySQL/SQLite instead of
  PostgreSQL timestamp values.

## Findings from advanced SQL example graduation

Building `examples/postgres-family-advanced-sql`,
`examples/mysql-family-advanced-sql`, and `examples/sqlite-family-advanced-sql`
graduated the Markdown contracts into workspace-checked examples while keeping
every missing primitive visible.

| Contract                         | Families                                          | Current implementation                                                                                                    | v0.8 analysis / fix target                                                                                                    |
| -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 01 ETL rollup                    | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb, sqlite/libsql             | Builder-native `insert().select()` + `filter()` + `dateTrunc()` + upsert.                                                 | Add expression aliases so repeated filtered aggregates and derived metrics are named once and reused safely.                  |
| 02 Window analytics              | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb; sqlite/libsql when probed | Parameterized raw SQL with `avg(...) over`, `rank()`, partitions, and frames.                                             | Add core `over()`, window specs, frame builders, ranking helpers, and capability gates.                                       |
| 03 Sessionization                | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb                            | Parameterized raw SQL with `lag()` and per-dialect gap math; SQLite skipped.                                              | Add `lag`/`lead`, portable date-diff/gap helpers, and SQLite semantics before claiming portability.                           |
| 04 Top-N per group               | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb; sqlite/libsql when probed | Parameterized raw SQL with `row_number()` in a derived table.                                                             | Add row-number/ranking helpers and a clean CTE/derived-table assembly seam.                                                   |
| 05 Cohort retention              | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb                            | Parameterized raw SQL with first-seen/activity CTEs; SQLite skipped.                                                      | Normalize cohort/date-bucket/date-diff semantics and alias reuse.                                                             |
| 06 Funnel analysis               | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb                            | Parameterized raw SQL over first-event CTEs; SQLite skipped.                                                              | Add first-event/funnel helpers or enough expression/alias primitives to build them without raw SQL.                           |
| 07 Recursive comments            | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb; sqlite/libsql when probed | Parameterized raw `WITH RECURSIVE`; dialect-specific path construction.                                                   | Add a recursive CTE builder plus depth/cycle guards and version-aware capability checks.                                      |
| 08 Job queue locking             | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb; sqlite/libsql CAS path    | Builder `.for("update", { skipLocked: true })` on row-locking engines; SQLite uses probed raw CAS `UPDATE ... RETURNING`. | Design a portable queue-claim/advisory-lock abstraction instead of making apps branch on locking style.                       |
| 09 Idempotent backfill           | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb                            | Builder rollup plus raw checkpoint upsert; SQLite skipped.                                                                | Define checkpoint/watermark contracts and failure-direction tests before `@sisal/etl`.                                        |
| 10 JSON table extraction         | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb; sqlite/libsql when probed | Parameterized raw `jsonb_to_recordset`, `JSON_TABLE`, or `json_each`.                                                     | Add JSON-table/set-returning function IR with typed projected columns and version gates.                                      |
| 11 Generated columns and indexes | pg/neon, mysql/mariadb, sqlite/libsql when probed | Raw DDL; MySQL partial indexes intentionally omitted as impossible.                                                       | Add generated columns, expression indexes, partial indexes, and typed unsupported-combination errors to schema snapshots/DDL. |
| 12 MySQL compatibility           | mysql/mariadb                                     | ODKU renders builder-native; base MySQL `RETURNING` renders a typed guard.                                                | Move version/variant capabilities into one descriptor consumed by `dialectGuard`, matrix generation, examples, and adapters.  |

Cross-cutting pain points from the graduation:

- **Raw SQL is safe but too necessary.** The examples use parameterized `sql`
  for windows, recursive CTEs, JSON-table projection, and richer DDL. v0.8
  should reduce these to small escape hatches, not make downstream packages
  assemble whole statements by hand.
- **Capability probing is duplicated.** SQLite/libSQL optional paths need local
  probes for JSON/window/generated/`RETURNING` support. v0.8's capability
  descriptor should make examples, guards, and the feature matrix read the same
  source of truth.
- **Generated DDL is still table-column-only.** Generated columns, partial
  indexes, expression indexes, and MySQL's lack of partial indexes are outside
  schema snapshots, so examples must issue raw DDL.
- **Write-result semantics remain leaky.** MySQL `RETURNING`, ODKU affected-row
  behavior, and SQLite CAS `UPDATE ... RETURNING` all point to a future
  "inserted/claimed/conflicted" result abstraction.
- **Statement assembly is the key package-boundary decision.** The examples can
  render advanced SQL today, but ETL/analytics packages need a stable assembler
  or core compile target that does not depend on ORM internals.

### Post-implementation status (2026-07-02)

A review of the two advanced-SQL example families against these contracts, after
waves 0–4, confirmed (by rebuilding each `"raw"` case with the shipped
primitives and rendering it) that the graduation's fix targets are **now
builder-native** for contracts **01–07** and the **index half of 11**:

| Contract              | Fix target                          | Now provided by                                                               |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 01 ETL rollup         | expression aliases                  | `expr()` (item 7) + assembly seam (5/13)                                      |
| 02 Window analytics   | `over()`/frames/ranking             | `over`/`WindowSpec`/`rank`/… (item 10)                                        |
| 03 Sessionization     | `lag`/date-diff                     | `lag` + `dateDiff` (item 10), SQLite-verified                                 |
| 04 Top-N per group    | `row_number` + derived-table seam   | `rowNumber` + `over` + `.as()` (item 10)                                      |
| 05 Cohort retention   | date-bucket/date-diff + alias reuse | `dateTrunc`/`dateDiff`/`expr` (9/10)                                          |
| 06 Funnel analysis    | first-event helpers / alias reuse   | `filter(min(...))` + `dateAdd` + `expr`                                       |
| 07 Recursive comments | `WITH RECURSIVE` builder            | `$withRecursive` (item 11)                                                    |
| 11 (index half)       | expression + partial indexes        | `index().on(sql\`…\`).where(…)` — snapshot + pg/sqlite DDL; mysql fail-closes |

**After wave 5**, the remaining v0.8 tracking items are closed too: contract
**10** (JSON set-returning) → item 14; the **generated-columns half of 11** →
item 15; and the **ODKU assignment-order** hazard (contract 12 pressure) → item
19. Contracts **08** (portable lock/claim) and **09** (checkpoint contract)
remain routed to **v0.9**. The example families still carry `"raw"`
implementations and stale `v08PainPoint` annotations for the now-solved cases;
refreshing them to builder-native is logged in
[v0.9](v0.9.0-roadmap.md#required-examples--tests).

## Open questions

- ~~**Compose-only fragment IR, or add an introspectable expression AST?**~~
  **Decided (wave 1, 2026-07-02): defer the AST, ship the seam** — the fragment
  IR stays the compile target, and the additive extension point is **shipped**:
  an opaque `meta` slot on `SqlChunk`
  (`SqlChunkMeta`/`withSqlChunkMeta`/`sqlChunkMeta`), renderer-ignored and
  carried by reference through composition, proven by the round-trip fixture
  `packages/orm/sql_meta_seam_test.ts` (annotations survive the `sql` tag and
  builder assembly with byte-identical render output). A transformable AST can
  populate the slot later as a non-breaking version bump — v0.13 DuckDB pushdown
  is unblocked either way (audit Fix 3 satisfied).
- ~~Expose the statement assembler publicly, or require downstream packages to
  compile fragments + a thin assembly helper?~~ **Decided (wave 1, 2026-07-02):
  expose a minimal assembly API from `@sisal/core`** — deterministic
  assemble-from-parts functions (select / insert-from-select with group-by and
  upsert), **not** the fluent OLTP builder, which stays in `@sisal/orm`.
  Rationale: fragments-only would force ETL/analytics to hand-assemble whole
  statements (the "raw SQL is too necessary" pain point), while exposing the
  fluent builder would drag the OLTP surface into the compile target. The G1
  dependency direction holds: `@sisal/etl` / `@sisal/analytics` depend on core
  only. The wave-4 core-only fixture (item 13) may use exactly this API, nothing
  more.
- ~~Does `@sisal/migrate` depend on `@sisal/core` only, or still on
  `@sisal/orm`?~~ **Decided (wave 1, 2026-07-02): core only.** Everything
  `@sisal/migrate` consumes (the snapshot contract in `schema.ts`, errors, the
  render primitives) lives in the extracted lower tier; no OLTP-builder
  dependency exists to keep. The wave-2 extraction re-points it, and the
  import-migration notes record the change. Coupled decision, same day:
  **checkpoint-table ownership stays `@sisal/etl`-managed**
  (`sisal_etl_checkpoints`, the v0.6 A3 default, ratified per audit Fix 2) — no
  `etl → migrate` edge is added, `architecture.md`'s graph is already correct,
  and v0.9 validates the contract per engine (item 18 closed).

## Acceptance criteria

- `@sisal/core` exists, is published to **JSR** (npm deferred — see the
  [npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md)), and
  `@sisal/orm`/adapters import from it; the public ORM API is unchanged for
  users (`@sisal/orm/core` stays as a compatibility re-export of `@sisal/core`).
- Every IR construct (select/CTE/insert-from-select/aggregate/window where
  supported) has golden per-dialect SQL tests and correct parameter binding.
- A downstream package can construct and render a grouped insert-from-select +
  filtered aggregate using only `@sisal/core` exports (proven by a fixture).
- The transformable-AST decision is documented (build / defer / reject) **and,
  if deferred, the additive extension point is shipped and proven** by a fixture
  that round-trips a fragment through the seam — so v0.13 cannot be blocked by a
  frozen IR.
- The capability descriptor's key is **`(engine, variant, version-range)`** —
  the v0.7 `DialectIdentity` `(engine, variant, version)` axis generalized with
  version-range predicates. It must express all six matrix/integration
  capability targets (`pg`, `neon`, `sqlite`, `libsql`, `mysql`, `mariadb` —
  neon ≠ pg and libsql ≠ sqlite as well as the MySQL/MariaDB variant split), and
  `dialectGuard` is generalized to a version-aware capability check (e.g. MySQL
  ≥ 8.0.21 for `RETURNING`, Postgres ≥ 14 for `CYCLE`, SQLite ≥ 3.31). It is the
  single source the matrix, `dialectGuard`, and ETL/analytics read.
- The **statement-assembly seam decision is closed** — exposed minimal assembly
  API or fragments-only — documented, and reflected in the core-only fixture
  (which may use exactly what the decision makes public, nothing more).
- The **`@sisal/migrate` dependency decision is closed** (core-only vs
  `@sisal/orm`) and reflected in the extraction's import graph and the migration
  notes.

## Affected packages

New `@sisal/core`; `@sisal/orm`, `@sisal/migrate`, `@sisal/pg`, `@sisal/neon`,
`@sisal/sqlite`, `@sisal/libsql`, `@sisal/mysql` (re-point imports). No
app-visible API break.

## Required examples / tests

- Golden-SQL suites per dialect for all IR constructs.
- A `@sisal/core`-only fixture compiling the v0.6 `post_hourly_stats` rollup
  SQL.
- Prepared-plan round-trip tests.

## Dialect compatibility notes

Capability gates become **declarative** (an `(engine, variant, version-range)`
capability descriptor — keyed to express the six matrix targets, **not** the
4-value render dialect, which still collapses neon→postgres and libsql→sqlite)
rather than scattered render branches. It is the single source the v0.9 matrix
and the `dialectGuard` both read. Window/data-modifying-CTE/insert-from-select
rendering is Postgres-first with explicit gates for the SQLite and MySQL
families.
```

### docs/v0.9.0-roadmap.md

URL: https://gilvandovieira.github.io/sisal/v0.9.0-roadmap.html

```md
---
title: v0.9.0 Roadmap
---

# Sisal v0.9.0 roadmap — Adapter Hardening, Observability & Capability Matrix

With the IR stabilized in [v0.8](v0.8.0-roadmap.md) and **MySQL added in
[v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md)**, v0.9 hardens the **five** adapters against the
ETL/analytics SQL shapes surfaced by
[v0.6](v0.6.0-roadmap.md)/[v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md), and turns the implicit,
scattered dialect knowledge into an **explicit, tested capability surface**.
This is the last hardening step before the public `@sisal/etl` (v0.10) and
`@sisal/analytics` (v0.11) packages are built on top.

## Status legend

| Symbol | Meaning                                                    |
| ------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| 🔴     | Not started · 🟡 In progress · 🟢 Done — move to CHANGELOG |

## Priority summary — everything to track

All v0.9 tasks, consolidated from this document's phased execution tasklist. The
phases below remain the dependency-compatible build sequence; this table is the
release-tracking view.

| ID  | Task                                                            | Stream                  | Priority | Effort | Status |
| --- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | -------- | ------ | ------ |
| T1  | Logging verbosity — tests complete                              | Observability           | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T2  | `await using` — extend to adapter migrator facades              | Lifecycle               | P2       | S      | 🟢     |
| T3  | Per-cell matrix wiring from `capabilitySupported(...)`          | Capability descriptor   | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T4  | Reconcile descriptor key spaces + version axis                  | Capability descriptor   | P1       | L      | 🟢     |
| T5  | `@sisal/pg` `float8`/`float4` → JS `number` (shipped v0.5.0)    | Adapter correctness     | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T6  | Broad numeric/date round-trip suite                             | Adapter correctness     | P1       | L      | 🟢     |
| T7  | Resolve the `bigint` cross-adapter split                        | Adapter correctness     | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T8  | MySQL functional-index version gate                             | MySQL/MariaDB hardening | P2       | M      | 🟢     |
| T9  | MySQL/MariaDB `RETURNING` + index gaps as capability rows       | MySQL/MariaDB hardening | P2       | M      | 🟢     |
| T10 | Per-engine fallback integration tests                           | Adapter correctness     | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T11 | Portable advisory-lock / queue-claim abstraction                | ETL substrate           | P1       | L      | 🟢     |
| T12 | Atomic load+advance invariant                                   | ETL substrate           | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T13 | Validate checkpoint/watermark contract                          | ETL substrate           | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T14 | Replay-vs-retention refusal                                     | ETL substrate           | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T15 | Write-result abstraction — inserted vs conflicted/claimed       | ETL substrate           | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| T16 | CTE per-engine execution tests                                  | Execution verification  | P2       | M      | 🟢     |
| T17 | Recursive-CTE per-engine/version verification                   | Execution verification  | P2       | M      | 🟢     |
| T18 | Insert-from-select / data-modifying CTE per-engine verification | Execution verification  | P2       | M      | 🟢     |
| T19 | Refresh advanced-SQL example families to builder-native         | Examples/docs           | P2       | L      | 🟢     |
| T20 | Update compatibility docs + unified matrix                      | Examples/docs           | P1       | M      | 🟢     |

**P1 spine.** The v0.10-critical path is T3/T4 and T11–T15. T4 must land before
version-gated capability work such as T8 and before ETL consumers read the
descriptor. T5–T10 make adapter truth test-backed, while T16–T18 pin the v0.8
constructs on real engines. T20 closes the release once the tests and capability
rows reflect the hardened behavior. Phase 6 remains explicitly deferred and
should not appear as active v0.9 scope.

## Code-state audit (2026-07-02)

The tasklist was re-baselined against the **actual code** (four parallel audits
over `integration/`, `packages/`, `examples/`, `docs/`) after T5 turned out to
be already shipped in v0.5.0. The status table above reflects the corrections:

- **Already done (false 🔴 → 🟢):** **T5** (float8→number, shipped v0.5.0) and
  **T10** — every degraded path (`ilike`→`LIKE`, `FILTER`→`CASE`,
  `dateTrunc`→text, bucket math) already has per-engine assertions in
  `integration/_shared/*_scenarios.ts`.
- **More done than marked (→ 🟡, small slices remain):** **T6** (float +
  date/Temporal cover all six targets; only typed `numeric`/`bigint` round-trips
  missing); **T7** (pg↔neon `bigint` divergence already pinned in
  `cross_adapter_parity_test.ts` + documented as an open decision — the
  _decision + coercion_ and extending the pin remain); **T9** (RETURNING surface
  fully declared/tested/matrix'd; only the index limits → registry rows and
  `IF NOT EXISTS` remain); **T16** (a non-recursive `WITH` runs on 5/6 engines
  inside the mutation-joins scenarios; a dedicated read-CTE scenario + MariaDB
  coverage remain); **T18** (insert-from-select covered on all six; only the
  sqlite/libsql data-modifying-CTE guard scenario missing); **T20** (matrix +
  `mysql-compatibility.md` current; pg/neon/sqlite/libsql compat docs a
  half-step behind).
- **Less done than marked (🟡 → 🔴, greenfield):** **T13**
  (`sisal_etl_checkpoints` exists only in a design doc + an example demo table)
  and **T15** (v0.8 recorded the _decision_; no `WriteResult` type — results
  carry only `rowCount`/`insertId`).
- **Confirmed 🔴:** **T8** (expression indexes rejected unconditionally; the DDL
  generator takes no version/identity — the base-engine-scoped version gate it
  needs, "MySQL ≥ 8.0.13 but not MariaDB", was **not expressible** in the guard
  model. That expressiveness (`baseEngine` on `unless`) has since shipped, so T8
  is now **unblocked**; only the DDL generator's version-gating remains);
  **T11/T12/T14** (ETL substrate, design-only — the migration-internal locks and
  `db.batch`/`defineAtomicOperation` primitives exist but satisfy none of the
  tasks); **T17** (zero recursive-CTE integration coverage); **T19** (only 1–2
  of 11 advanced-SQL example contracts migrated to builder-native; the rest
  still raw with stale `v08PainPoint` notes).

**Revised picture.** Done: T1–T5, T9, T10 (7 — T9 completed same-day: partial
index promoted to a registry capability). Partial/near-done: T6, T7, T16, T18,
T20 (5 small slices). Not-started: T8, T11–T15, T17, T19 (8 — the ETL substrate
is the bulk and the v0.10 gate). The critical path (T11–T15) is unchanged, now
confirmed greenfield.

**Since this audit (Phase 2 closed).** The adapter-correctness + MySQL-gate
slices have landed: **T6** (typed `numeric`/`bigint` round-trips + a
`valueShape.bigint` contract), **T7** (pg int8 → string; the Postgres family now
aligns on precision-preserving strings), and **T8** (functional-index version
gate via the `baseEngine` predicate) are 🟢, and Phase 3 is underway: **T11**
(portable advisory lock, a lightweight lock-row lease), **T12** (atomic
load+advance via `etlCheckpoint` — TEXT watermarks, `db.batch` atomicity), and
**T13** (checkpoint-contract validation + the A3 ownership record), **T14**
(replay-vs-retention refusal — `prune`/`assertReplayable`), and **T15** (the
`tryInsert`/`WriteOutcome` write-result abstraction, now consumed by the T11
lock) are 🟢 — **the Phase 3 ETL substrate (T11–T15, the v0.10-critical path) is
complete.** Phase 4 execution-verification then landed: **T16** (read-CTE
scenarios), **T17** (recursive-CTE scenarios — which uncovered and fixed a
latent invalid-SQL bug in the builder-native recursive step, now guarded), and
**T18** (data-modifying-CTE guard) are 🟢, **T20** (compatibility docs + the
unified matrix refreshed to the v0.9-hardened reality) is 🟢, and **T19** (the
advanced-SQL example families migrated from raw to builder-native across
pg/mysql/sqlite) is 🟢. **All of v0.9 (T1–T20) is now complete.** The status
table above is authoritative; this audit is retained as the dated re-baselining
record.

## Goal

Make each adapter a known quantity for ETL/analytics: every dialect-divergent
behavior is **declared, documented, and tested**, with explicit fallbacks where
an engine can't match Postgres.

## Scope / focus

- **PostgreSQL** — the strongest ETL/analytics target (insert-from-select,
  FILTER, windows, advisory locks, materialized views); the reference adapter.
- **Neon** — Postgres-compatible but **serverless-aware** (WebSocket Pool;
  prefer generated SQL / atomic statements). Separate generic-Postgres behavior
  from Neon-specific behavior in code and docs.
- **SQLite** — embedded/local compatibility and fallbacks (`FILTER`→`CASE WHEN`,
  no materialized views, lock-row instead of advisory locks).
- **libSQL** — SQLite semantics + **remote/Turso runtime caveats** (hrana/HTTP,
  `ArrayBuffer` BLOBs, `intMode`).
- **MySQL / MariaDB** — harden the v0.7 adapter: `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE`
  upsert, uneven `RETURNING`, `BOOLEAN`=`TINYINT(1)`, `GET_LOCK` named locks,
  and the MySQL-vs-MariaDB split.
- **Transaction behavior differences**; **returning/upsert differences**;
  **timestamp/date function differences** (the v0.6 bucket abstraction per
  dialect); **lock/checkpoint strategy differences** (advisory lock vs lock
  row).
- **Operational observability** — Hibernate-style category/level logging for
  SQL, bind summaries, migration plans/steps/locks/history, and CLI verbosity,
  so adapter hardening has inspectable diagnostics without leaking secrets.
- **Test coverage for dialect-specific fallbacks** — every degraded path
  (FILTER→CASE, ilike→LIKE, bucket math) gets a per-engine integration test.

## Specific hardening items (from the audit)

- **Declarative `(engine, variant, version-range)` capability descriptor** (the
```
